refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' clause which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague; 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is more precise and solution-neutral. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the acceptance criterion context without prescribing how it is achieved.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate across the communication channel. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and loss statistics.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE teleoperation communication channel shall reliably transmit operator commands to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE teleoperation communication channel shall reliably transmit equipment state and sensor feedback to the operator.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency anomalies) to the operator and safety systems.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.

DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy ratios, tuned to customer network conditions during commissioning, to tolerate packet loss without requiring retransmission.

DP4: Implement a prioritized message queue with quality-of-service (QoS) differentiation, buffering critical control commands separately from telemetry, and dropping non-critical packets under congestion.
DP5: Establish a closed-loop acknowledgment and timeout-driven retransmission protocol with exponential backoff, allowing the system to adapt retry behavior to observed network latency.

DP6: Combine network condition monitoring (loss rate, jitter, latency estimation) with dynamic codec/payload compression to reduce packet size and inherent loss probability under constrained bandwidth.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 (hybrid): Implement adaptive packet redundancy via selective retransmission (DP1) for critical control commands, combined with application-level forward error correction (DP3) for telemetry and non-critical feedback. Network quality metrics (loss rate, latency) drive real-time tuning of redundancy ratios and retransmission thresholds. This hybrid approach decouples the solution from specific network technologies while remaining solution-neutral and allowing customer-defined packet loss targets to be met through configurable parameters.

potential_sources: Coupling between retransmission timeout logic and actual network latency characteristics—mitigated by continuous latency estimation and adaptive backoff.
Coupling between FEC redundancy overhead and available bandwidth—mitigated by dynamic ratio adjustment based on observed loss rate.
Coupling between command criticality classification and application domain logic—mitigated by explicit QoS tagging at the application boundary, not embedded in the communication layer.
Coupling between customer-defined loss targets and system tuning parameters—mitigated by parameterization and commissioning-time calibration, not hard-coded thresholds.
coupling_check.notes: The hybrid DP maintains logical independence by separating concerns: retransmission handles command integrity, FEC handles telemetry robustness, and network monitoring drives adaptation. No single mechanism is overloaded. The design is decoupled from specific network types (WiFi, cellular, wired) and allows independent evolution of each strategy.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network conditions are measurable and stable enough during operation to allow real-time metric collection (loss rate, latency) without excessive overhead.
ASSUMPTION: Acceptable latency budget exists for retransmission cycles; if round-trip time is very high (e.g., satellite links), retransmission may be impractical and FEC-only may be required.
ASSUMPTION: Application can tolerate occasional command loss if retransmission fails; if zero-loss is mandatory, a higher redundancy ratio or dual-channel DP2 may be necessary.

RISK: FEC overhead increases packet size; under severe bandwidth constraints, this may paradoxically increase loss probability. Mitigation: dynamic ratio tuning and fallback to DP2 (dual-channel) if FEC overhead exceeds threshold.

RISK: Adaptive tuning introduces state and complexity; incorrect parameter selection during commissioning could degrade performance. Mitigation: provide commissioning tools and telemetry dashboards to validate tuning.

RISK: Retransmission introduces latency jitter; safety-critical commands may require bounded latency guarantees not provided by this DP alone. Mitigation: coordinate with worksite safety plan to define acceptable command latency bounds and classify commands accordingly.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable latency budget for command delivery (e.g., 'command must be delivered within 500 ms'), (2) classification of commands by criticality (e.g., 'emergency stop must be delivered with <1% loss; telemetry can tolerate 5% loss'), and (3) expected network conditions during commissioning (e.g., 'WiFi 5 GHz in industrial environment, typical RTT 20–50 ms'). This will allow DP selection and IM packaging to be optimized for the specific operational context and will clarify whether DP1+DP3 is sufficient or whether DP2 (dual-channel) is required for safety compliance.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Adaptive packet redundancy and forward error correction logic for hybrid network resilience, including real-time network quality monitoring, redundancy ratio tuning, retransmission threshold management, and FEC codec selection. Encapsulates both critical command path (selective retransmission) and telemetry path (application-level FEC) under unified network quality-driven adaptation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives critical control commands from operator; Teleoperation applies selective retransmission policy before transmission via Network Link

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state and sensor data; Teleoperation applies application-level FEC encoding before transmission via Network Link

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network transport; Teleoperation observes loss rate and latency metrics to drive real-time tuning of redundancy ratios and retransmission thresholds

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Critical safety alerts bypass standard FEC and use selective retransmission; Teleoperation routes safety-critical packets through high-redundancy path

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of bidirectional command/feedback resilience logic. Hybrid DP (DP1+DP3) directly addresses operator-to-vehicle and vehicle-to-operator reliability. Network quality metrics are observable at this layer. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert routing may require coordination with Safety Services for priority handling, but FEC/retransmission logic remains contained.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns vehicle state, actuators, and onboard control logic. Placing network-layer redundancy and FEC here creates semantic mismatch—Vehicle should not own teleoperation protocol resilience. High diffusion: command reception and telemetry transmission logic would need to diffuse into Teleoperation anyway, creating bidirectional coupling and split ownership of network resilience.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety policy and critical alert generation, not network transport resilience. Placing hybrid redundancy/FEC here conflates safety logic with network engineering. High diffusion: selective retransmission for critical commands and FEC for telemetry are orthogonal to safety decision-making; ownership would be split across Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating tight coupling.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP1+DP3 here violates the principle of assigning logic to the most specific boundary. All lower-level modules would depend on NGHE Solution for network resilience, creating excessive coupling and preventing independent testing/evolution of Teleoperation resilience.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The hybrid DP (selective retransmission for critical commands + application-level FEC for telemetry) is fundamentally a teleoperation-layer concern: it bridges operator intent (Tasking) and vehicle feedback (Telemetry) across an unreliable network. Network quality metrics (loss, latency) are observable at the Teleoperation boundary, enabling real-time tuning without requiring Vehicle or Safety Services to be aware of network conditions. Configurable packet loss targets and redundancy ratios remain solution-neutral and customer-definable within Teleoperation's scope.
containment_risks: SafetyAlert routing: If Safety Services generates critical alerts that must bypass standard FEC and use high-redundancy retransmission, Teleoperation must coordinate with Safety Services to identify and prioritize safety-critical packets. Risk: unclear ownership of alert transmission policy. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface contract specifying that Teleoperation applies selective retransmission to all SafetyAlert messages without exception.

Network Link abstraction: If Network Link interface does not expose loss rate and latency metrics in real-time, Teleoperation cannot observe network quality to drive adaptive tuning. Risk: DP3 (real-time tuning) becomes unimplementable. Mitigation: ensure Network Link interface includes bidirectional telemetry of network quality metrics (loss %, latency percentiles) updated at configurable intervals.

Codec/algorithm selection: If FEC codec or retransmission algorithm must be swapped at runtime based on network conditions, Teleoperation must own codec registry and selection logic. Risk: tight coupling to specific FEC libraries. Mitigation: define abstract FEC and retransmission strategy interfaces; Teleoperation selects strategy based on network quality, but does not implement codecs directly.

Vehicle-side reception: Vehicle module must correctly receive and validate redundant/FEC-encoded packets. Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation may have mismatched understanding of packet format. Mitigation: define packet format contract (redundancy markers, FEC metadata) in shared interface specification.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target) is measurable and testable at the Teleoperation module boundary. Network quality metrics (loss rate, latency) are observable via Network Link interface. Hybrid DP (DP1+DP3) tuning parameters (redundancy ratios, retransmission thresholds) are configurable and traceable to customer-defined packet loss limits. End-to-end verification can be performed by injecting controlled packet loss into Network Link and validating that Teleoperation maintains loss ≤ target through adaptive redundancy and FEC.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR requirement (maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits) maps unambiguously to Teleoperation IM boundary. Teleoperation owns both critical command path (selective retransmission via DP1) and telemetry path (application-level FEC via DP3). Ownership is semantically coherent: teleoperation resilience is a teleoperation-layer concern, not distributed across Vehicle, Safety Services, or top-level NGHE Solution. Interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, Network Link bidirectional, SafetyAlert out) are well-defined and support the DP implementation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (hybrid DP1+DP3) directly and sufficiently addresses FR. Selective retransmission (DP1) handles critical control commands; application-level FEC (DP3) handles telemetry and non-critical feedback. Real-time network quality monitoring and adaptive tuning of redundancy ratios and retransmission thresholds provide the mechanism to meet customer-defined packet loss targets under agreed network conditions. DP is solution-neutral (does not mandate specific network technology or FEC codec) and remains configurable. No logical gap between FR intent and DP mechanism.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert routing coordination: SafetyAlert interface specifies that critical safety alerts bypass standard FEC and use selective retransmission. Risk: unclear ownership of alert transmission policy and priority handling between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly stating that Teleoperation applies high-redundancy selective retransmission to all SafetyAlert messages without exception, and that Safety Services is responsible only for alert generation, not transmission policy.

Network Link metric exposure: DP3 (real-time tuning) requires Network Link to expose loss rate and latency metrics in real-time. Risk: if Network Link interface does not provide bidirectional telemetry of network quality metrics, Teleoperation cannot observe network conditions to drive adaptive tuning, rendering DP3 unimplementable. Mitigation: ensure Network Link interface contract includes loss rate (%), latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99), and update frequency (e.g., per-packet or per-window) as mandatory telemetry.

FEC codec and retransmission strategy abstraction: If FEC codec or retransmission algorithm must be swapped at runtime based on network conditions, Teleoperation must own codec registry and selection logic. Risk: tight coupling to specific FEC libraries or algorithms; difficult to evolve or test independently. Mitigation: define abstract FEC strategy and retransmission strategy interfaces; Teleoperation selects strategy based on network quality metrics but does not implement codecs directly. Codecs are pluggable implementations.

Vehicle-side packet format contract: Vehicle module must correctly receive, decode, and validate redundant/FEC-encoded packets. Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation may have mismatched understanding of packet format, redundancy markers, or FEC metadata, causing silent failures or packet rejection. Mitigation: define shared packet format specification (redundancy markers, FEC metadata, sequence numbers, checksum fields) in Tasking and Telemetry interface contracts. Include packet format validation tests in integration suite.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural and semantically correct owner of bidirectional command/feedback resilience logic. Hybrid DP (DP1+DP3) is fully contained within Teleoperation boundary: selective retransmission for critical commands, application-level FEC for telemetry, network quality monitoring, and real-time tuning of redundancy ratios and retransmission thresholds all belong to the teleoperation layer. No diffusion into Vehicle (which should not own network protocol resilience) or Safety Services (which should not own network transport logic). Minor diffusion risk (0.08) is limited to SafetyAlert routing coordination, which is manageable via interface contract. Teleoperation does not duplicate or scatter DP1+DP3 logic across multiple modules; logic is cohesive and localized.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Network Link interface contract to mandate real-time exposure of network quality metrics (loss rate %, latency percentiles, update frequency). Without this, DP3 (real-time tuning) cannot be implemented or verified.

Define SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly: Teleoperation applies high-redundancy selective retransmission to all SafetyAlert messages. Safety Services is responsible for alert generation only; Teleoperation is responsible for transmission policy and priority handling.

Introduce abstract FEC strategy and retransmission strategy interfaces to decouple Teleoperation from specific codec implementations. Teleoperation selects strategy based on network quality metrics; codecs are pluggable.

Define shared packet format specification (redundancy markers, FEC metadata, sequence numbers, checksums) in Tasking and Telemetry interface contracts. Include packet format validation tests in integration suite to prevent Vehicle-side decoding failures.

Add acceptance criterion for Network Link metric latency: network quality metrics must be updated and available to Teleoperation within [X] milliseconds to enable responsive adaptive tuning. Without this, real-time tuning may lag behind actual network conditions.

Document configurable parameters (redundancy ratios, retransmission thresholds, FEC codec selection criteria) as part of Teleoperation module specification. Ensure parameters are customer-definable and traceable to packet loss target acceptance criterion.

decision.justification: Gate results show all three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence (verification_feasibility: High, allocation_clarity: Clear, containment_notes: 0.92 score). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that critical interface contracts and implementation abstractions are not yet formalized. Given high safety criticality, proceeding directly to implementation without decomposing these interface and strategy concerns into explicit sub-FRs would create unmanaged integration risk. Specifically: (1) Network Link metric exposure is a prerequisite for DP3 (real-time tuning) but is not yet contractually guaranteed; (2) SafetyAlert routing coordination requires explicit ownership and priority handling logic that should be decomposed as a separate FR; (3) FEC strategy abstraction and packet format validation are architectural concerns that warrant explicit sub-level treatment to ensure pluggability and testability. ZIGZAG_DOWN allows formalization of these concerns as child FRs before detailed design, reducing integration risk and improving verification traceability.
next_level_sub_frs: FR 2.1: Expose real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency percentiles, update frequency) from Network Link to Teleoperation module to enable adaptive redundancy and FEC tuning.
FR 2.2: Route and prioritize safety-critical alerts (SafetyAlert messages) with high-redundancy selective retransmission, independent of standard telemetry FEC encoding.

FR 2.3: Implement pluggable FEC strategy and retransmission strategy selection logic that adapts codec and algorithm choice based on observed network quality metrics without tight coupling to specific codec implementations.

FR 2.4: Validate and enforce shared packet format specification (redundancy markers, FEC metadata, sequence numbers, checksums) between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules to prevent silent decoding failures.

revise.why: Current DP (hybrid DP1+DP3) is logically sound but lacks explicit decomposition of interface contracts and strategy abstraction. Revise DP to explicitly state: (1) DP1 (selective retransmission) includes SafetyAlert priority handling as a distinct sub-mechanism; (2) DP3 (real-time tuning) depends on Network Link metric exposure contract; (3) FEC codec selection is abstracted via pluggable strategy interface, not hardcoded; (4) packet format validation is a shared contract enforced at Tasking/Telemetry boundaries. This revision ensures DP is implementable and verifiable without ambiguity about interface dependencies and architectural abstractions.

assumptions: Network Link interface can be extended to expose real-time loss rate (%), latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99), and update frequency (per-packet or per-window) without breaking existing Vehicle or Safety Services dependencies.
SafetyAlert messages are generated by Safety Services and routed through Teleoperation; Teleoperation owns transmission policy and priority handling, not Safety Services.
FEC codec implementations (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) are available as pluggable libraries or can be abstracted via strategy interface without performance penalty.
Vehicle module can be updated to correctly decode redundant/FEC-encoded packets if packet format specification is provided and validated in integration tests.

Customer-defined packet loss target (≤ 1% or alternative) is achievable under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G with specified coverage and congestion profiles) using hybrid DP1+DP3 without requiring additional network infrastructure investment.
open_risks: Network Link metric latency: if Network Link metric updates lag behind actual network conditions by >100ms, Teleoperation adaptive tuning may be reactive rather than proactive, reducing effectiveness. Mitigation: define acceptance criterion for metric update frequency (e.g., ≤50ms latency) in Network Link interface contract.

SafetyAlert priority inversion: if SafetyAlert messages are queued behind standard telemetry during high packet loss, safety-critical alerts may be delayed. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert queue priority and timeout handling explicitly in Teleoperation DP; ensure SafetyAlert messages bypass standard FEC queue and use dedicated high-redundancy path.

FEC codec selection complexity: if network conditions change rapidly (e.g., 4G to 5G handover), codec selection logic may oscillate or select suboptimal codec. Mitigation: define hysteresis and stability criteria for codec selection; include codec switching latency in DP specification.

Packet format version mismatch: if Vehicle and Teleoperation modules are deployed with different packet format versions, silent decoding failures may occur. Mitigation: include packet format version field in packet header; define version negotiation or compatibility checking in Tasking/Telemetry interface contract.

Integration test coverage: packet format validation and SafetyAlert priority handling require end-to-end integration tests with controlled packet loss injection. Risk: insufficient test coverage may miss edge cases (e.g., simultaneous SafetyAlert and high-priority command during extreme packet loss). Mitigation: define integration test matrix covering packet loss rates (0%, 1%, 5%, 10%), SafetyAlert frequency, and command frequency combinations.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification document with mandatory real-time metric exposure (loss rate %, latency percentiles, update frequency, metric latency SLA).
SafetyAlert interface contract defining Teleoperation ownership of transmission policy, priority handling, queue management, and timeout behavior.
FEC strategy and retransmission strategy interface definitions (abstract base classes or trait definitions) with pluggable codec registry and selection logic.
Shared packet format specification document (redundancy markers, FEC metadata, sequence numbers, checksums, version field) with packet format validation test suite.

Teleoperation module specification documenting configurable parameters (redundancy ratios, retransmission thresholds, FEC codec selection criteria, SafetyAlert priority levels) and their traceability to customer-defined packet loss target.
Integration test plan covering packet loss injection, SafetyAlert priority validation, codec switching behavior, and packet format compatibility across Vehicle and Teleoperation module versions.

Network condition simulation environment (e.g., network emulator or testbed) capable of injecting controlled packet loss, latency, and jitter to validate DP1+DP3 effectiveness under agreed network conditions.
